tagline: When treatment is a dose, not a switch, parallel trends must hold across the entire intensity distribution.
selection_problem: The naive comparison E[Y|D=d1] - E[Y|D=d2] confounds the dose effect with selection bias: units choosing higher doses may have different baseline trajectories. Bias equals E[Y_i(0)|D=d1] - E[Y_i(0)|D=d2], which is nonzero if dose correlates with pre-trends.
counterfactual_problem: We never observe what high-dose units would have earned or produced at a lower dose in the post period, since each unit receives exactly one dose level.
po_setup: Define Y_i(d,t) as the potential outcome for unit i under dose d in period t, with D_i in [0,∞) the continuous treatment intensity. The estimand is the dose-response ATT: E[Y_i(d,1) - Y_i(0,1) | D_i=d] as a function of d.
po_intuition: The PO framework forces us to see that we need a counterfactual trend for every dose level d, not just treated vs. untreated. Regression with a linear dose term implicitly assumes a constant marginal effect, hiding heterogeneity in ATT(d) across the distribution.
solution: Identification exploits pre-post variation within units and across dose levels, using zero-dose or low-dose units as the counterfactual trend for high-dose units. The counterfactual is the trajectory low-dose units followed, scaled to what high-dose units would have done absent treatment.
key_insight: Parallel trends must hold conditional on dose, so any pre-existing correlation between dose and trend is fatal — not just correlation between treatment status and trend.

assumptions.name: Dose-Parallel Trends
assumptions.formal: E[Y_i(0,1) - Y_i(0,0) | D_i=d] constant in d
assumptions.intuition: If high-dose units were on steeper pre-trends, the DiD estimate conflates trend differences with dose effects.

assumptions.name: No Anticipation
assumptions.formal: Y_i(d,0) = Y_i(0,0) for all d and pre-period t
assumptions.intuition: If units ramp up activity before treatment arrives, pre-period outcomes already reflect treatment, poisoning the baseline.

example_setup: A government subsidizes energy-efficiency retrofits in 2021, with subsidy size D_i varying continuously across firms by size. Outcome Y_i is energy consumption. Larger firms select larger subsidies, and they may also be growing faster.
example_counterfactual: We cannot observe how much energy a high-subsidy firm would have consumed in 2021 had it received zero subsidy.
example_execution: The method differences out pre-period energy trends within each firm and then compares the post-minus-pre change across firms at different subsidy levels. Firms receiving near-zero subsidies provide the counterfactual trend for high-subsidy firms.
example_result: A 10,000 USD increase in subsidy reduces energy use by 4.2 MWh, representing Y_i(d) vs Y_i(0) holding pre-trends constant.
source_title: Heterogeneous Treatment Effects and Bias in the Linear DiD Model with Continuous Treatment

math_panel.label: Dose ATT
math_panel.note: Causal effect at each dose level

did_shape.caption: High-subsidy firms (treated) fall below their counterfactual trend post-2021; low-subsidy firms provide the trend.
